planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after last frost; prefers rocky or gravelly soil with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in full sun in well-drained, lean soil; this subshrub suffers in heavy clay or waterlogged ground and thrives on neglect. Shear lightly after the distinctive cone-shaped flowers fade to maintain a compact mound and encourage fresh aromatic growth. Avoid overhead watering and do not fertilize heavily, as rich soil dulls the intensely oregano-like fragrance that makes this herb prized in Greek and Middle Eastern cuisines. In zones 7–8, mulch the crown lightly before hard freezes; in zones 9–10 it grows virtually year-round with minimal care.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring once soil has warmed; space generously to promote air circulation around the mounding gray-green foliage.

directions: Establish rooted cuttings in fast-draining, alkaline to neutral soil in a location with maximum sun exposure, mirroring its native Spanish hillside habitat. Water sparingly once established; the camphor-rosemary-marjoram scent is most concentrated in dry conditions and will diminish if the plant is overwatered or grown in shade. Trim back by one-third after the white fluffy flower heads fade to keep the plant bushy and to prevent it from becoming overly woody at the base. Protect from prolonged hard freezes with a cloche or cold frame, as it is less cold-hardy than common thyme.

planting.note: Set out cuttings after last frost in lean, well-drained soil; avoid amended beds that encourage lush but less flavorful growth.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in full sun and gritty, low-fertility soil to replicate the rocky Iberian slopes where this thyme naturally carpets the landscape. Harvest small-leaved stems frequently throughout the growing season to encourage branching and to capture the delicate, slightly floral thyme flavor before the plant flowers. After blooming, shear plants back by about half to prevent woodiness and to stimulate a flush of fresh growth for late-season harvesting. This species forms a tidy, low mat suitable for edging herb beds or tucking into gaps in stone walls.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings after last frost into sharply draining soil; this Levantine subshrub demands full sun and resents any standing moisture at the roots.

directions: Establish plants in the sunniest, driest spot in the garden, as this Eastern Mediterranean subshrub is evolved for calcareous, rocky soils and intense summer heat; poor drainage is the primary cause of failure. Harvest leafy stem tips before or just as the whorled pink-purple flowers open, when the essential oil content peaks, then dry in bunches to use in homemade za'atar spice blends. Trim lightly after each harvest and cut back by a third following the main flowering period to maintain a bushy form and prevent the base from becoming too woody. In zone 7, provide a light mulch around the crown in winter and site plants against a south-facing wall for additional warmth.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after all frost danger has passed; this heat-loving shrub establishes fastest when nighttime temperatures remain above 55°F.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in full sun and well-drained soil after the last frost date; this tropical shrub grows vigorously in heat and humidity that would stress common oregano, making it an excellent substitute in warm-climate gardens. Water deeply but infrequently once established, allowing the soil to dry out between sessions, as the plant is highly drought-adapted and prone to root rot in soggy ground. Harvest stem tips regularly throughout the warm season, as frequent cutting promotes dense branching and keeps the intensely oregano-scented leaves at peak potency for culinary use. In zone 8, mulch heavily around the base before the first frost and cut plants back to a few inches in late winter; they will resprout vigorously from the woody base as temperatures climb.
